entry.title: Re: How in SAP S/4 HANA public cloud legal requirement fo...
entry.content:
1. Tax Determination in Purchase Orders (PO)
Tax Service Integration: When you create a PO, the system calls the SAP Localization Hub – Tax Service.

Condition Technique: The PO line item carries material tax classification (e.g., Apparel, Footwear, Luxury). The net price per unit is evaluated against the threshold scales defined in condition records.

Default Tax Code: The tax code is not manually chosen; instead, it is defaulted by the Tax Service based on:

  • Product category
  • Transaction value (per piece/pair)
  • Threshold scales (Rs. 500, Rs. 1,000, Rs. 2,500, etc.)

Result: The PO shows the correct GST % (5%, 12%, 18%, or 40%) depending on the threshold.

2. Goods Receipt (GR)
At GR, tax is not recalculated — the system simply carries forward the tax determination from the PO. The GR document ensures consistency in valuation and tax postings, but the tax code itself is inherited from the PO line item.

3. Invoice Receipt (MIRO / Supplier Invoice)
Tax Revalidation: During invoice posting, the Tax Service is called again to validate the tax code against the transaction value. If the supplier invoice matches the PO value, the tax code remains consistent. If there’s a mismatch (e.g., supplier charges higher value per piece, crossing a threshold), the Tax Service can re-determine the tax code:

Example: PO created at Rs. 2,400 per piece (5% GST), but invoice shows Rs. 2,600 → system re-determines tax as 12% (or 18% in 2025 framework).

Compliance Reporting: Advanced Compliance Reporting ensures that the correct GST slab is applied and reported to authorities.

4. How Tax Code Gets Defaulted
Tax Code Mapping: Each GST rate (5%, 12%, 18%, 40%) is mapped to a tax code in the system (e.g., I1 = GST5, I2 = GST12, I3 = GST18, I4 = GST40).

Defaulting Logic: The Tax Service determines the applicable rate. The system automatically assigns the corresponding tax code to the PO/invoice line item.

No Manual Intervention: End users don’t need to manually select tax codes; they are defaulted based on condition records and thresholds.

entry.title: Re: How do we get open items only of gl vendor and custom...
entry.content:
The correct way to identify open items is not to rely only on BSIS, but to use line item reports (FBL1N, FBL3N, FBL5N) or tables like BSIK/BSID (open items) vs. BSAK/BSAD (cleared items).

When moving to S/4HANA, you don’t migrate all historical line items — only open items and balances. The recommended approach is:

Step-by-Step

1) Identify Open Items in ECC

  • Use transaction codes:
    • FBL1N (Vendor Open Items)
    • FBL5N (Customer Open Items)
    • FBL3N (GL Open Items)
  • Or extract from tables:
    • BSIK/BSID → Open items
    • BSAK/BSAD → Cleared items (exclude these)
  • Run program RFSEPA01 or RFITEMAP/AR/GL to extract open items.

2) Reconcile with Balances

Ensure that the sum of open items matches the balances in GL accounts. This avoids discrepancies after migration.

3) Data Migration Cockpit (DMC) in S/4HANA

  • Use SAP’s Data Migration Cockpit templates for:
    • G/L Open Items
    • Customer Open Items
    • Vendor Open Items
  • Upload only open items (from step 1 extraction).

4) Load into S/4HANA

DMC loads open items into FI line item tables (ACDOCA). Cleared items are not migrated; only balances are carried forward.

Validation

Run FBL1N/FBL5N/FBL3N in S/4HANA to confirm only open items are present. Compare totals with ECC reports.

Handling the BSIS Issue

  • If cleared items still appear in BSIS:
    • Run program RFSEPA03 (Reorganization of Index Tables) in ECC before migration.
    • This cleans up inconsistencies between BSIS and BSAS.
  • After cleanup, extract open items again to ensure accuracy.

entry.title: Close case
entry.content:
There is a case in our queue with status 'not sent to SAP', it can be closed now but I cannot close it.

How to close this case? thanks


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Close case
entry.content:
Dear customer,

The case with status 'Not sent to SAP' is Draft case.

If the draft case is not in use any more, it can be deleted on customer side, and kindly know the following S-users can delete Draft cases:

  • S-user ID that created the draft case
  • Super Administrators listed in My Important Contacts

Only Super Administrators can delete draft cases for other S-users; Cloud and User Administrators cannot delete draft cases created by other S-users at this time.

This is documented in KBA 2594949 - How to delete a draft case - SAP for Me, and please refer to the KBA for more detailed information.

Hope the above information helps.

Thanks and regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Standard report or tile for invoicing plan
entry.content: Hello Lakshmipathi. Thank for your answer. It still find surprising that SAP didn't propose a standard report when in the note SAP recommands not to create custom report on tables FPLT and FPLA : "Sometimes customers want to use the status of an invoicing date ('A' or 'C') for some report of their own. This status has to be calculated newly whenever it is needed. The status that is stored in table FPLT is irrelevant! The design is to calculate the status newly whenever the invoicing plan is touched. The system does this by taking the sum of all invoices that belong to this item. This sum is then being distributed on the invoicing dates in chronological order (leaving out the invoicing dates with billing block). All invoicing dates that get at a part of the sum, get status 'C' ("partially processed / fully processed"). The invoicing dates that get nothing of the sum of all invoices, get status 'A' ("not processed"). -> when the status is needed for a new report, it must not (!!) be taken from the database but must be calculated! " So I let this thread open a little expecting someone gives another answer

entry.title: Solving Industrial Procurement's "Zero Results" Problem with Agent-to-Agent Communication on SAP BTP
entry.content:
What and Why did we build it?

We built this as a technology demonstrator to demystify modern AI systems. Complex multi-agent applications often feel like black boxes—even to technical people. By making every step visible through real-time graph visualization, this prototype serves as an educational tool that shows exactly how these systems work.

While not intended as a commercial product, we believe this level of transparency is exactly what's needed to build trust in enterprise AI adoption. Seeing is believing—and when you can watch agents discover each other, negotiate, and reach decisions in real-time, AI becomes understandable rather than mysterious.

Note: While this article uses industrial procurement as a concrete example, the principles and architecture described here apply to any application that needs to connect two sides of a marketplace—from healthcare provider discovery to freelance talent matching. The "Zero Results" problem exists wherever two parties need to discover each other.

A procurement manager needs high-strength reinforcement steel bars with specific tensile properties—Grade 500 MPa, 20mm diameter, corrosion-resistant coating for a coastal bridge project. A search query like "Grade 500 rebar 20mm corrosion-resistant custom specs" returns zero matches.

The procurement portal shows standard inventory. Email chains to suppliers take days. Manual verification is slow and unscalable.

This is the "Zero Results" problem—not just in industrial procurement, but in any domain where two parties need to discover each other. Traditional search engines and APIs excel at finding standard inventory, but they struggle when you need to find who can fulfill custom requirements.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has transformed AI applications, but standard vector-based RAG systems face the same limitation. They can find similar products, but they can't help connect the right parties.

The solution? Agent-to-Agent (A2A) communication—where autonomous agents discover each other, communicate, and negotiate without human intervention.

The Problem: The bottlenecks of Traditional Procurement

Current procurement tools hit a fundamental wall:

1. Keyword Search (SEO) Struggle with Specificity

  • Search engines match words, not needs
  • "Grade 500 rebar" might return generic product catalogs or academic papers
  • Custom specifications have no standard search vocabulary

2. Static APIs Only Handle Known Inventory

  • REST APIs return catalogs of existing stock
  • They can't answer: "Can you provide this?"
  • No mechanism for dynamic queries

3. Manual Processes Are Inefficient

  • Procurement managers manually email potential suppliers
  • Each supplier must independently verify feasibility
  • Days or weeks to get a response

The core issue? These systems treat procurement as information retrieval when it should be connecting the right parties.

The Solution: Autonomous Agent-to-Agent Discovery

We built an Autonomous Procurement System to solve this problem. It's a multi-agent system where autonomous AI agents discover, communicate, and negotiate with each other—no human intervention required until the final approval.

Here's how it works:

  1. User submits a natural language query via the UI
  2. Procurement Agent semantically searches a registry for relevant suppliers
  3. Registry Service returns matching supplier agent endpoints using HANA vector search
  4. Supplier Agents evaluate feasibility and provide quotes
  5. User receives structured quotes with supplier details and pricing

Why This is Different

Unlike traditional systems that search for products, this system searches for agents that represent suppliers. It's the difference between searching for: "Grade 500 rebar" and "Who can sell custom reinforcement steel to these specifications?"

Standing on the Shoulders of Giants

Before diving deeper, we want to acknowledge the innovations this builds upon:

  • The A2A Protocol: A standardized way for AI agents to discover and communicate with each other, pioneered by Google Cloud
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP): A protocol for connecting AI agents to external tools, created by Anthropic
  • SAP HANA Cloud: The only database we know that combines high-performance vector search with a native graph engine under one roof

This project extends these protocols specifically for the enterprise procurement use case, with a focus on preventing AI hallucinations through hybrid search.

Under the Hood: The Multi-Agent Architecture

The system consists of five main components working together:

1. Procurement Service

Role: The orchestrator—handles user requests and coordinates agent communication
Key Tech: LangGraph for transparent agent reasoning + A2A Protocol

Why it matters: LangGraph gives full control over agent decision-making, making every step traceable and auditable and the A2A Protocol allows communication with the suppliers.

2. Procurement UI

Role: Natural language interface for users
Key Tech: React + SAP UI5 Web Components

3. Registry Service

Role: Semantic agent discovery—the "Yellow Pages" for supplier agents
Key Tech: Vector embeddings for semantic search

Why it matters: Understands meaning, not just keywords. Finds "rebar suppliers" when you search for "reinforcement steel providers."

4. Supplier Agent

Role: Represents a supplier
Key Tech: A2A Protocol + MCP integration

Why it matters: Each supplier runs their own autonomous agent. Self-contained, independently operated.

5. Product Database (MCP Server)

Role: Supplier's internal catalog
Key Tech: MCP (Model Context Protocol)

Why it matters: Standardized tool access prevents hallucinations—agents only offer what they actually have.

How It Works: A Simple Example

Let's see this in action with a real-world scenario.

The Request

A project manager opens the interface and types a simple message:

"I need Grade 500 MPa reinforcement steel bars, 20mm diameter, with corrosion-resistant coating for a coastal bridge project. Need 10,000 units."

That's it. No forms, no technical specifications to fill out, no browsing through catalogs.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

The system springs into action automatically:

Step 1: Understanding the Need

The procurement agent reads the message and extracts the key requirements—high-strength steel, specific diameter, corrosion protection, quantity needed.

Step 2: Finding Relevant Suppliers

The agent searches the registry for suppliers who can fulfill these requirements. It's not matching keywords—it's understanding meaning. A supplier described as "marine-grade materials specialist" gets matched even though they never used the exact words in the query.

The registry returns relevant suppliers like:

  • Coastal Steel (specializes in marine-grade materials)
  • Advanced Rebar Solutions (custom fabrication with epoxy coatings)

Step 3: Agent-to-Agent Negotiation

The procurement agent contacts each supplier's agent directly. These conversations happen autonomously—no humans involved yet.

Each supplier agent checks their internal systems: Do we have the right materials? Can we meet the specifications? What's our current capacity?

Step 4: Getting Real Quotes

Within seconds, responses come back:

"Yes! We can provide Grade 500E rebar with epoxy coating, 20mm diameter. Quote: $2.85 per unit for 10,000 units ($28,500 total). Lead time: 3 weeks. Marine-grade certification included."

The crucial part? These aren't hallucinations. Each quote comes from real products in real databases.

One click to accept. The system handles the rest.

Compare that to the traditional process: days of emails, phone calls, and manual verification.

The Innovation: Transparent Multi-Agent Communication

The real breakthrough isn't just that agents can talk to each other—it's that you can see everything that happens.

Traditional systems are black boxes. You submit a request and get a result, but you have no idea what happened in between. Did it check the right suppliers? Did it understand your requirements correctly? Why did it choose that particular option?

Our system provides complete transparency through real-time graph visualization:

What You Can See:

  1. Discovery Phase: Watch the procurement agent query the registry and see which supplier agents are discovered

  2. Negotiation Phase: See the actual messages exchanged between the procurement agent and each supplier agent—not just the final answer, but the reasoning process

  3. Decision Path: Understand exactly why certain suppliers were contacted, what they offered, and how the final recommendations were made

  4. Message Content: Every query, every response, every tool call made by agents—fully traceable

Why This Matters:

Trust: When you can see the entire process, you trust the results. No more "AI magic" that nobody understands.

Debugging: If something goes wrong, you can trace exactly where and why. Did the agent misunderstand the query? Did a supplier agent fail to respond? It's all visible.

Auditing: For enterprise applications, compliance and auditing are critical. The graph shows the complete audit trail of every decision.

Learning: Watch how agents communicate and improve your system. See patterns in how successful negotiations happen.

This level of transparency is rare in multi-agent systems. Most implementations hide the complexity. We expose it—because transparency builds trust, and trust is essential for enterprise adoption.

The Future: Decentralized Agent Networks

This prototype uses a centralized registry, but the vision is much bigger:

1. Public Agent Registries

Imagine a decentralized "Yellow Pages" where any supplier can register their agent.

2. Standard Protocols

The A2A Protocol is evolving. Future versions might use technologies to verify agents which will pave the way for secure, verifiable agent communication.

3. Cross-Industry Application

While this prototype demonstrates procurement, the architecture applies to any two-sided marketplace:

  • Healthcare: Finding specialized medical equipment or connecting patients with specialists
  • Aerospace: Sourcing custom components with specific tolerances
  • Electronics: Discovering chip fabrication services
  • Professional Services: Matching clients with freelancers based on specific skill requirements
  • Real Estate: Finding properties with custom specifications
  • Logistics: Discovering carriers with specific route and cargo requirements

4. Automated Transactions

Add payment rails, agents could autonomously execute purchases within approved budgets.

Conclusion: Beyond Procurement

While this Blog uses industrial procurement as the example, the core insight applies broadly: the future of any two-sided marketplace isn't about better keyword search—it's about autonomous agents discovering each other.

Any application that needs to connect buyers with sellers, clients with service providers, or requesters with providers can benefit from this architecture. The principles remain the same:

  • Semantic discovery through vector embeddings
  • Agent autonomy through standard protocols (A2A, MCP)
  • Hallucination prevention through grounded data
  • Enterprise reliability through integrated infrastructure (SAP BTP, HANA Cloud)

The "Zero Results" problem exists wherever two parties need to discover each other. Agent-to-Agent communication solves it.

Resources

  • Tech Stack: SAP AI Core | SAP HANA Cloud | React | Node.js | Python
  • Protocols: A2A SDK | MCP Docs

Built with SAP AI Core, SAP HANA Cloud, React, and the A2A Protocol

entry.title: Re: Code to show a F4 Help for RFC Server Group in custom Dynpro or Selection screen
entry.content:
In RZ12, the code is based on an internal logic (PROCESS ON VALUE-REQUEST and internal subroutine) with a dedicated Dynpro screen, that you could copy, but there are simpler solutions like in the transaction ATC > Schedule Runs:

 

The value help shows "2" which means that the RFC server group is assigned 2 application servers.

The input field is typed according to the data element SATC_D_SERVER_GROUP which is linked to the Search Help SATC_F4_SERVER_GROUP.

 

PARAMETERS p_rfcgrp TYPE satc_d_server_group.

 

For information:

The ABAP code selects all the lines from the table RZLLITAB, which also contains Logon Server Groups defined by the transaction SMLG. The column differentiates the two types of server groups via the column GROUPTYPE: space for Logon, 'S' for RFC.

Another solution is to use SCI_DY270-SGROUP which is linked to the Search Help SERV_GROUP with the parameter GROUPTYPE = 'S', but you won't see the number of servers per group:

ABAP code:

 

PARAMETERS p_rfcgrp TYPE sci_dy270-sgroup.

 

Definition of SCI_DY270-SGROUP:

This answer is valid for S/4HANA 2023 FPS01, ABAP 7.58 SP 1.

entry.title: Re: How to know programmatically the pragma of a given syntax message?
entry.content:
In the ABAP statement SYNTAX-CHECK, there's an internal addition ID 'LTXT' which is not documented. The lines of the internal table must be of type RSLINLTXT, e.g.

 

DATA o_error_descriptions TYPE TABLE OF rslinltxt.
DATA warnings_table       TYPE TABLE OF rslinlmsg.
DATA correction_table     TYPE TABLE OF sedi_correction_table.
DATA error_table          TYPE TABLE OF rslinlmsg.

      SYNTAX-CHECK FOR l_environment
        MESSAGE o_error_message
        LINE    o_error_line
        OFFSET  o_error_offset
        WORD    o_error_word
        PROGRAM source_name
        DIRECTORY ENTRY l_trdir_source_entry
        FRAME ENTRY l_trdir_rahmen_entry
        INCLUDE o_error_include
        MESSAGE-ID o_message_id
        ID 'CORM' TABLE correction_table
        ID 'MSG'  TABLE warnings_table
        ID 'ERR'  TABLE error_table
        ID 'LTXT' TABLE o_error_descriptions.

 

o_error_descriptions (ID 'LTXT'):

Warnings_table (ID 'MSG'):

The column MESSAGE for the message ID MESSAGE G2L is stored in the table TRMSG.

The long text is stored in the table DOKTL, in the line ID = 'TX' and OBJECT = 'TRMSG_MESSAGE_G2L'.

The text introducing the pragma ("This message can be handled [...] highlighted)") is in the table DOKTL, in the line ID = 'TX' and OBJECT = 'INCL_ABAP_PRAGMA'.

For information, here are the other possible IDs, from the ABAP Keyword Documentation SYNTAX-CHECK ... ID id TABLE idt ...:

System: ABAP 7.58 SP 1.

entry.title: Re: Why SELECT returns different rows when querying a CDS View on MSEG versus MSEG directly
entry.content:
It's a feature. In fact, the replacement object is rarely used as documented:

  • When accessing a DDIC database table or a DDIC database view with a replacement object assigned, the access is redirected to the replacement object in the following cases:
    • When used as a data source of a SELECT statement in ABAP SQL. This also applies to subqueries in any ABAP SQL statement and to the statements OPEN CURSOR and FETCH. If table buffering is defined for the DDIC database table or the DDIC database view in question, this table buffer is bypassed.
    • When a foreign key relationship is checked for Web Dynpros or dynpros.
  • All other access types are still performed on the DDIC database table or DDIC database view ...

If you try to update MSEG via ABAP SQL, you get this warning (ID "MESSAGE G2L" in the table TRMSG):

  • The table MSEG has a replacement object and hence should no longer be modified.

But you don't get any warning if MSEG is used in a CDS View. Too bad.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Create Custom Analytical Query in SAP S/4HA...
entry.content:
Good day @Octavio_Ruiz4 

Thank you for your question and the details provided.

The reason why the create button is disabled could be because:

1. You are using the old app where creation is no longer supported and SAP has moved the creation to Custom Analytical Queries and Services. This is mentioned in KBA 3718160.

2. Your role has the needed catalogs but the key catalog for query design is assigned without write access. So, you cannot “Create” because the authorisation is missing.

To solve the authorisation issue,

  • Ensure your catalogue SAP_BW_BC_AQD_PC is assigned with write access. For more details see KBA 3653452
  • If you still do not have access to write after that, ensure your IAM Apps activation for the role.

see the reference below on how to activate IAM Apps

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/how-to-activate-or-deactivate-iam-apps-app-authorization-variants?locale=en-US 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: I finally found a use for AI in Datasphere
entry.content:
Enjoyed this post, painfully recognizable 😅

We ran into exactly the same issue. Since most of our customer’s reports are in Dutch, we ended up building our own workaround. We created local tables with Dutch quarter/month/day names, unioned them with the existing date tables, and built new text views and a custom Date dimension on top.

Bonus: we could immediately enrich the dimension with working days, public holidays, and proper Dutch business names.

Not perfect, but at least manageable. Would be great if SAP eventually made this unnecessary 😉

entry.title: LDAP Based User Management in SAP Cloud Connector
entry.content:
In this blog I will explain about how we can use LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) to manage Cloud Connector users and authentication.

After installation of the Cloud Connector, it uses file-based user management by default.

With file-base user management we can login with Administrator user.

Alternatively, the Cloud Connector also supports LDAP based user management.

With LDAP based user management, users are authenticated against your company’s LDAP directory service. We can check from the below screen as user store shows LDAP.

Advantage of using LDAP based user management:

LDAP based user management can authenticate the users based on provided roles. These roles can be used to differentiate between users who are only allowed to view the configuration and those who are responsible for the administration functions.

Below are the steps to activate LDAP for user administration in SAP Cloud Connector:

We need to connect with our LDAP team to get the below details:

  •  LDAP Server name and port – Default port is 389, but if we want to use secure connection then port 636 is being used. Make sure ports are open from cloud connector server to LDAP server.
  • Root and Intermediate certificates of LDAP server– This need to be imported in Cloud Connector side.
  • SCC user account details that can login to LDAP server.
  • LDAP group sccadmin for SAP Cloud Connector - A role is a group in LDAP terms. The Cloud Connector provides the following roles: sccadmin, sccsupport, sccmonitoring and sccdisplay.

Once we get the above details we can start as per below steps:

Step 1: Login to SAP Cloud Connector with Administrator user and navigate to Configuration. Under User Interface tab select the Switch to LDAP button as mentioned in below screenshot:

Step 2: Provide the LDAP server's host and port (port 389 is used by default) in the <Host> field. To use the secure protocol variant LDAPS based on TLS, select Secure and user port 636.

Step 3: Provide a service user and its password in the fields Connection Username and Connection Password.

Step 4: In the Search Rules configuration, the most important point is to mention the correct syntax and attribute based on your LDAP configuration settings. Below is the example which I used in my scenario.

  • roleBase: groups information we can get it from LDAP team, where they have created the roles.
  • userbase: users information we can get it from LDAP team, where they have created the users or where the users are located.
  • userRoleName="memberOf”: This information we can get it from LDAP team, which syntax is for user role name.
  • userSearch="(sAMAccountName={0})”: This information we can get it from LDAP team, which syntax is for user search. This is the preferred attribute often used for Active Directory, sAMAccountName, the corresponding Search Rules configuration is userSearch="(sAMAccountName={0})". For example, userSearch="(uid={0})" can also be used if uid attribute is being used in LDAP.
  • roleSubtree="true": group nodes on the LDAP server may be located under several branches inside the "base" branch. In this case, add the boolean attribute roleSubtree="true".
  • userSubtree="true": user nodes on the LDAP server may be located under several branches inside the "base" branch. In this case, add the boolean attribute roleSubtree="true".

Step 5: Below are the available roles that can be assigned to users based on the requirement:

  • sccadmin: This role has full administrative rights in the cloud connector.
  • sccdisplay: This role enables the display of all data in the cloud connector.
  • sccsupport: This role is used to display data and support activities (e.g., to activate traces).
  • sccmonitoring: This role is required to access the monitoring APIs of the cloud connector.

Below is the example for setting up custom roles (sccadmin) in cloud connector

Step 6: Set up the LDAP Trust Store. When we are configuring LDAP for User, the LDAP Trust Store can be set up by adding certificates to the Allowlist and choosing a Trust Option.

There are three trust options:

  • Trust all: All certificates are trusted. This is the least secure option which does not consider any trusted certificates, in the allowlist or otherwise, and simply trusts all certificates.
  • Use LDAP trust store only: Only the certificates listed in the allowlist are trusted.
  • Use JDK and LDAP trust store: The certificates in the allowlist and those of the JDK are trusted.

An LDAP connection over TLS can cause TLS errors if the LDAP server uses a certificate that is not signed by a trusted CA. We can use the keytool utility for importing the certificates which we have received from LDAP team. Usually, the trust storage location is cacerts inside the java directory (jre/lib/security/cacerts).

Below is the command for importing the certificate:

"C:\SAP\sapjvm-8.1.107\sapjvm_8\jre\bin\keytool.exe" -import -trustcacerts -file "D:\Cert_Chain\certificate.cer" -alias certificate -keystore "C:\SAP\sapjvm-8.1.107\sapjvm_8\jre\lib\security\cacerts" -storepass changeit

Step 7: Once all the above steps are completed save the configuration as draft and test the configuration by using the below button:

Step 8: Once test LDAP configuration is successful, Activate the configuration. This will restart the cloud connector.

Step 9: After the restart of cloud connector, we can login to cloud connector with user as per company’s LDAP directory service.

Note:

In case of any issue in Test LDAP configuration, kindly check with LDAP team for the correct syntax and attribute for userbase and rolebase. You can also install ldp on the cloud connector server and check the connectivity and binding with the service users and find out the correct attributes.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1: What's in it for the Utilities Industry?
entry.content:
The first feature pack (FPS1) of the SAP S/4HANA 2025 has just been released to customers. Below is an overview of its key innovations tailored specifically for the utilities industry:

1. Enterprise search for utilities master data
Using the Enterprise Search from Fiori Launchpad, you can now search various utilities-specific master data - such as premise, utility installation, point-of-delivery, device and device information record. Starting from the search results,  you can directly access frequently used apps and transactions for each master data entity. This intuitive approach significantly reduces search time and enhances operational efficiency. 

2. Graphical display of utilities interval data
A new Fiori app Display Utilities Time Series Values (F8929) is available for SAP S/4HANA Utilities, designed for the Meter Data Specialist (Utilities) role. This app delivered detailed insights into time series values from EDM profiles through a graphical representation that combines the values with related status information.

3. Archiving of utility installations and premises and deletion of unused device information records 
New archiving object ISU_EANL is now available to archive utility installations, including their related installation structure and points-of-delivery. Additionally, the archiving object ISU_EVBC enables the archiving of premises. Especially the first one (ISU_EANL) comprises comprehensive validations for dependent data, making sure that all related documents and master data entities are archived beforehand. Furthermore, a new report REG_DEV_INFO_REC_CLEANUP, has been introduced to delete unused device information records. 

4. More flexible completeness check for profile values
Based on customer feedback from the SAP Continuous Influence session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities, transaction EEDMPROFVALCHECK (Check Whether Profile Values Are Complete) has been enhanced:

  1. The meter reading unit and service type of the utility installation are now available as additional selection criteria.
  2. Existing BAdI ISU_BI_RTP_STATUS_CHECK method STATUS_CHECK_EXT now provides more precise information regarding the billability of profile values.

5. Additional CDS views for time series data

SAP S/4HANA Utilities now includes multiple CDS views for time series data extraction, supporting various interval lengths, status fields, and related attributes. For details on these new CDS views, refer to the What's New.

6. Enhancements in  SAP Market Process Management

The What's New  section in the SAP Help Portal outlines several enhancements related to SAP Market Process Management.  For full details, please refer to the following link: What's New . You can find utilities-related content in a dedicated section under Industries > Energy & Natural Resources.

Finally - here is a list of previous SAP Community blog posts, beginning with release SAP S/4HANA 2023:

 SAP S/4HANA release
(and feature pack)		Available
since		 
2023Oct 2023SAP S/4HANA 2023: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS1Feb 2024SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS1: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS2Oct 2024SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS3Feb 2025SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS3: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2025Oct 2025SAP S/4HANA 2025: What's in it for the Utilities Industry?

entry.title: Re: Account assignment in Purchase requistion (MTO)
entry.content:
Good day @Moon3 

Thank you for your question and the details provided.

In an MTO flow that starts from a sales quotation, the system can keep the quotation as the origin reference for the requirement and the resulting planning or procurement objects. This explains why your PR shows the quotation number even though a sales order exists. See KBA 3360455 and KBA 2856701 for more details.

In the Public Cloud, you can avoid PR being created from quotation origin by:

  • Ensuring MRP and procurement are triggered only when the sales order requirement is the driver.
  • Creating the PR so that it references the correct SD document after the sales order exists.

From a standard point of view, you cannot switch to automatically convert an already created PR reference from quotation to sales order without re creating it. This would require a custom logic.

Regarding your question on how you can do it?

Answer: Do not generate PRs from quotation-based requirements. Rather, trigger planning and PR creation only after the sales order exists, and recreate the PR if it was created earlier with a reference to a quotation.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Fieldglass Rates 2.0: Revolutionizing Rate Management for Modern Workforce Solutions
entry.content:
Hi there and welcome to my latest blog. 

My name is Fabian Caelers, I have recently joined the Solution Value Advisory team (SVA) for SAP Fieldglass.  Prior to joining I gained extended experience working as part of the SAP Fieldglass Delivery Team, as well as working for an MSP program exclusively for a client.  During that time, I have realized how maintenance-heavy rates and related admin objects in SAP Fieldglass can be.  Therefore, I decided to spotlight the Rates 2.0 feature with this blog.

In today's dynamic business environment, organizations managing contingent workforces and service contracts face increasingly complex rate structures that can quickly become difficult to maintain.  SAP Fieldglass Rates 2.0 emerges as a game-changing solution that transforms how companies handle rate management, offering unprecedented simplicity without sacrificing functionality.

This comprehensive functionality addresses the growing complexity of external workforce management while maintaining the powerful capabilities that SAP Fieldglass users have come to rely on.

What is SAP Fieldglass Rates 2.0?

SAP Fieldglass Rates 2.0 represents a fundamental reimagining of rate management within the SAP Fieldglass Vendor Management System.  At its core, Rates 2.0 is a newly developed rate structure that dramatically reduces configuration complexity while preserving all the comprehensive functionality of the traditional Rates 1.0 system.

The solution addresses a critical challenge that many organizations face: as rate management requirements become more sophisticated—involving multiple suppliers, varying pay rates, location-specific regulations, and complex billing structures—the number of rate objects can increase exponentially, making administration cumbersome and error-prone.  Rates 2.0 solves this by introducing a streamlined approach that can reduce millions of rate objects down to thousands, making rate management far more efficient and maintainable.

How Rates 2.0 Works

  1. Custom Lookup Foundation: Instead of relying on complex Rate Groups and Rate Grids, Rates 2.0 uses Custom Lookups as the primary mechanism for rate determination.  These dynamic tables incorporate multiple conditions such as role, location, and suppliers, significantly reducing the number of required administrative objects.
  2. Streamlined Rate Structure Creation: The system creates a series of building blocks that combine to define your company's rate structure.  This includes Rate Categories, Rates, and Custom Lookups that work together to eliminate the need for traditional Rate Groups and Rate Grids.
  3. Automated Rate Selection: The lookup table performs the heavy lifting, automatically determining the correct rates based on predefined criteria.  This approach supports both pay rate versus bill rate structures and markup versus coefficient-driven calculations, while accommodating various worker premiums for overtime, unit pay, and benefits.

Benefits for Your Organization

Operational Efficiency: Organizations can experience dramatic reductions in administrative overhead.  One large customer reduced their rate objects from 2.3 million down to just 1,000 manageable objects, demonstrating the transformative potential of this approach.

Enhanced Flexibility: The system maintains all the powerful functionality of Rates 1.0 while providing greater flexibility in configuration.  Organizations can handle complex rate scenarios with custom logic applied across various parts of the SAP Fieldglass application.

Improved User Experience: The simplified structure makes rate management more intuitive and less time-consuming.  Users can focus on strategic workforce management rather than getting bogged down in complex administrative tasks.

Scalability: Rates 2.0 is designed to handle large datasets efficiently, making it suitable for organizations of all sizes, from medium-sized companies to very large enterprises with complex global operations.

Implementation Considerations

Prerequisites

Before implementing Rates 2.0, organizations must populate admin objects used as keys in Custom Lookups.  This includes ensuring that all currency codes, site codes, and other administrative objects are properly configured before populating the custom lookups.

Best Practices

  • Coexistence Strategy: Organizations currently using Rates 1.0 can implement Rates 2.0 alongside their existing structure, allowing for gradual migration without disrupting ongoing operations.
  • Data Preparation: Careful planning of Custom Lookup structures is essential to maximize the benefits of the simplified approach.
  • Training and Change Management: While Rates 2.0 is more user-friendly, proper training ensures teams can fully leverage the new capabilities.

Comparison with Legacy Rate Management

Aspect

Rates 1.0

Rates 2.0

Impact

Rate Objects

Up to 2.3 million objects

Reduced to ~1,000 objects

99%+ reduction in complexity

Configuration Method

Rate Grids and Groups

Custom Lookups

Simplified maintenance

Setup Requirements

Complex prerequisites

Streamlined enablement

Faster implementation

Maintenance Effort

High administrative overhead

Minimal ongoing management

Significant time savings

Why you should adapt this feature

SAP Fieldglass Rates 2.0 represents a significant leap forward in rate management technology, offering organizations the opportunity to dramatically simplify their rate structures while maintaining full functionality.  The transition from complex Rate Grids to intuitive Custom Lookups can transform how organizations manage their contingent workforce costs, reducing administrative burden and improving operational efficiency.

For organizations currently struggling with complex rate management or those looking to optimize their contingent workforce operations, Rates 2.0 offers a compelling path forward. The ability to coexist with Rates 1.0 ensures a smooth transition, while the simplified configuration process makes adoption accessible to organizations of all sizes.

The time to explore Rates 2.0 is now—as more organizations adopt this streamlined approach, those who delay risk falling behind in workforce management efficiency and capability.

I hope you like this blog and are looking forward to reading more content soon.

Best Regards,
Fabian

entry.title: Re: Table(s) for advanced foreign currency revaluation po...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA, the architecture is simplified. All postings, including valuation, are stored in the Universal Journal (ACDOCA). There is no separate FAGL_BSBW_HISTRY table anymore.

FAGL_FCV → The new transaction for Advanced Currency Valuation. Simulation Ledger / Extension Ledger → If you run valuations in simulation mode, results are stored in the extension ledger rather than a separate history table. Configuration: You define valuation areas and ledgers in customizing (FINSC_LEDGER, valuation methods).

Instead of querying FAGL_BSBW_HISTRY, you now:

  • Use FAGL_FCV to run and review valuation runs log.
  • Check postings directly in ACDOCA (filter by valuation area, document type, or ledger).
  • For audit/history, SAP provides valuation log tables and ACV run administration tables, but the actual postings are always in ACDOCA.

entry.title: Correction of Trading Partner in Intercompany Invoice Already Posted
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am currently facing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition  related to an intercompany invoice posted in Production.

Due to the configuration of the document type, specifically because the “Inter-Company” indicator was not activated, the system did not correctly derive the Trading Partner (GL Company ID) during posting.

The issue has now been identified and corrected in configuration (the Inter-Company indicator has been activated). However, the accounting document was already posted.

Situation:
• The document corresponds to an intercompany customer invoice.
• The Trading Partner field was correctly populated in the customer line item (Balance - group 4).
• However, it was not populated in the revenue G/L account (P&L account – group 7).
• As this is an intercompany transaction, the Trading Partner should also be informed in the revenue line.

Problem:

Since this is already a posted accounting document in Production, we need to understand whether it is possible to update or complete the Trading Partner field in the revenue line item without reversing and reposting the document.

I have reviewed the job:

“Post Balance Sheet Reclassification – Post Master Data Changes”

However, this job only applies to balance sheet accounts, and does not allow processing for P&L accounts, which is precisely where the correction is required.

Any idea?

Regards.

Álvaro

entry.title: Re: Eclipse: hana calculation view wrong visualizations
entry.content:
I have fixed the problem. I was upgrading to IDE 2025-12, but I had that problem. So I have installed version IDE 2025-09 and now it is working.

First, uninstall your previous version and then:

1. Download eclipse folder: https://www.eclipse.org/downloads/download.php?file=/technology/epp/downloads/release/2025-09/R/eclipse-java-2025-09-R-win32-x86_64.zip

2. Unzip the folder. An eclipse folder will be created. Move it wherever you want.

3. Execute eclipse.exe

4. You will be required to create a new workspace.

5. Install ABAP and HANA plugins.

6. Recover your perspectives and landscape.

Et voilá! Calculation view again with boxes and arrows...

entry.title: Impact of Activating KOSDL (Cost Center) in Document Splitting – S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear community,

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, we are reviewing the configuration under: Define Document Splitting Characteristic for Controlling

The characteristic KOSDL (Cost Center) is currently activated.

While testing, I have observed the following behavior:

When executing a complete procurement process (PO → Goods Receipt → Supplier Invoice → Payment), and each step is posted with a different posting date (therefore different exchange rates), the system generates an additional accounting entry at the payment stage that is not expected (see screenshots attached).

After deactivating the KOSDL check, this additional posting disappears, which technically makes sense.

However, before deciding to permanently deactivate it, I would like to understand the potential impact.

Context:
• Only one Profit Center in the system
• Only one Segment active

My concern is whether deactivating KOSDL (Cost Center) in document splitting could have side effects in other processes (e.g., reporting, consistency checks, future controlling scenarios, etc.).

Are there any risks or standard implications of disabling KOSDL in this scenario that we should consider before making the change permanent?

Thank you in advance for your insights.

Best regards,
Álvaro

entry.title: Re: Batch Management in advanced intercompany process
entry.content:
Good day @Hammer1 

Thank you for your question and the details provided.

Based on your description, it seems the automatic GIFS posting is trying to post with a batch reference but in the transit plant there is no batch master entry for that material and plant, so the posting fails and generates that error. KBA 2477479 describes this scenario.

Note that if you mix batch management across different plants in the same Advanced Intercompany flow, it will break once an automated step tries to post a batch in a plant where the batch master does not exist.

In Public Cloud, you must align batch handling so the automated postings can find a valid batch master record.

I am not aware of any Public Cloud customisation switch that tells the system to ignore batch checks for GIFS when the posting carries a batch. The batch master check is mandatory when you reference a batch.

The only recommended clean workaround I know is to make the transit plant batch managed for this material. You do not need to use batches operationally there, but the system must be able to hold the batch reference for the automated step.

Best regards

Chris

entry.content: Truly inspiring use case and E2E solution!

entry.title: Re: Batch Management in advanced intercompany process
entry.content:
Unfortunately, there is no customizing switch to bypass batch checks in GIFS. The supported approaches are:

Option A: Align Batch Management
Activate batch management in the transit plant for the material. This ensures batch data flows consistently and avoids XMCHA errors. This is the recommended SAP-supported approach.

Option B: Use Classic Intercompany Process
If you cannot activate batch management in the transit plant, consider using the classic intercompany process instead of the advanced scenario. Classic intercompany does not enforce the same level of batch consistency.

In on-premise systems, you could theoretically enhance the posting logic to skip batch checks. In Public Cloud, such modifications are not allowed — only extensibility via in-app or side-by-side extensions is possible, and this particular check cannot be bypassed.

entry.title: At-Least-Once Delivery (ALOD) in SAP Commerce: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
Overview

When we build integrations in SAP Commerce Cloud, especially for important business data like orders, reliability becomes very important. We cannot afford to lose messages just because an external system was temporarily down.

This is where the At-Least-Once Delivery (ALOD) feature really helps.

What is ALOD?

ALOD stands for At-Least-Once Delivery.

As the name says, it guarantees that outbound webhook events are delivered at least once. If a webhook call fails due to network issues or the external system being unavailable, SAP Commerce automatically retries sending the message for up to 24 hours. If the message still cannot be delivered after multiple attempts, it is moved to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) so that we can monitor and investigate it later.

So instead of losing data, we always have visibility and control.

Why Is This Important?

Let’s take a simple example. Suppose you need to send order data to an external system. In a traditional approach:

  • You write custom code to call the external API.
  • You implement retry logic manually.
  • If retries fail, the data is lost.
  • You only have logs to check what happened.

This approach works, but it adds complexity and risk.

With ALOD:

  • You publish an event instead of directly calling the external system.
  • The webhook listens to the event.
  • If delivery fails, the system retries automatically.
  • If it still fails, the message goes to the DLQ.

This makes the whole integration more reliable and easier to manage.

How ALOD Works in SAP Commerce Cloud

In SAP Commerce Cloud, ALOD works together with webhooks and Azure Queue. When ALOD is enabled for a webhook:

  • The event payload is stored in an Azure Queue.
  • The system processes it asynchronously.
  • If delivery fails, it retries automatically.
  • If undeliverable, it moves the message to a Dead Letter Queue.

For every ALOD-enabled webhook, a corresponding DLQ container is also created.

This gives us:

  • Automatic retry mechanism
  • Safe storage of failed messages
  • Ability to download and analyze undelivered messages

High-Level Implementation Steps

  1. Add Required Extensions
    • webhookservices
    • webhookbackoffice
  2. Assign Required Roles

    To access integration screens and DLQ monitoring in Backoffice, users need:

    • backofficeadministratorrole
    • deadletterqueuemonitoringgroup.     
    •  
  3. Create a Custom Business Event

    Create a custom event, for example CustomOrderExportValueEvent, and make sure it extends AbstractEvent so that it is available when generating the model class. This event should contain all the payload data that needs to be sent to the external endpoint. The POJO structure should be mapped using the standard approach through impex or API configuration.

        
  4. Configure Webhook and Enable ALOD

    Create a WebhookConfiguration and make sure the ALOD flag is enabled.     

  5. Trigger the Event

    Instead of calling the external system directly inside your order process:

    • Trigger the custom event.
    • The webhook configuration listens to it.
    • The message gets stored in Azure Queue.
    • Processing happens asynchronously.  

Important Points to Remember

1) Payload Size Limitation

  • Default payload limit in standard code: 20 KB
  • Azure Queue maximum limit: 64 KB
  • Payload size validation happens in AzureEventPersistentQueueService
  • If you need a bigger payload, you should customize carefully

2) It Is Asynchronous

ALOD processing is completely asynchronous. Your main business process does not wait for the external system response.

3) Adding Custom Headers

If you need to add extra headers to webhook requests, you can implement OutboundRequestDecorator. This helps you add custom key-value headers before the request is sent.

Final Thoughts

From my experience, ALOD is a very useful feature when dealing with important outbound integrations. Instead of writing complex retry logic and worrying about data loss, we can rely on the platform’s built-in eventing and queue mechanism.

It makes integrations:

  • More reliable
  • Easier to monitor
  • Cleaner in design
  • Less risky

If you are working on outbound integrations in SAP Commerce Cloud, enabling ALOD is definitely something you should consider.

Useful References

  • Webhooks Documentation
  • POJO Integration Objects
  • At-Least-Once Delivery
  • Creating a Webhook with a POJO Integration Object
  • Advanced Integration Object Modeling
  • Modifying Outbound Request

entry.title: Change installation name
entry.content:
Hi Team , 

As we have new systems DIGITS and DIGITS for that system we need help to change the installation name.

entry.title: Re: Impact of Activating KOSDL (Cost Center) in Document...
entry.content:
Potential impact of deactivating KOSDL

Reporting granularity: If you rely on document splitting to produce financial statements by cost center, deactivating KOSDL removes that capability. However, in most systems, cost center reporting is handled in Controlling (CO), not via document splitting in FI.

Consistency checks: Document splitting ensures that all dimensions are balanced. Without KOSDL, you’ll still have balancing by profit center and segment (since those are active), but not by cost center.

Future controlling scenarios: If you plan to use advanced reporting or reconciliation between FI and CO, having cost center as a splitting characteristic can help. If you don’t, it may be redundant.

Audit implications: Auditors sometimes expect document splitting to align with controlling objects. If cost center is not critical for your statutory or management reporting, disabling it is acceptable.

In your specific context

  • You mentioned only one profit center and only one segment. That means the main balancing dimensions are already very simple.
  • If cost center reporting is already managed in CO (standard practice), then keeping KOSDL active in document splitting adds complexity without real benefit.
  • Deactivating KOSDL in this scenario is unlikely to cause issues, provided you don’t need FI line items balanced by cost center for external reporting.

entry.title: Understanding the Regulatory Change of updates to Individual Income Tax Withholding System in China
entry.content:
What's Changing?

China’s State Taxation Administration updated the Individual Income Tax Withholding System (Golden Tax III) on January 23, 2026, introducing significant changes to the calculation and reporting of individual income tax. Although the Administration has not released official upgrade notes or a new user manual for these changes, the system’s calculation rules have been optimized, which impact cumulative deductions in the payroll process.

Key Changes to Standard Deductions

The most significant update involves how cumulative standard deductions are calculated for comprehensive income.

  • For income items including regular wages and salaries, insurance agent commission income, securities broker commission income, and other continuous labor remuneration income (regardless of employment or engagement status), the calculation rule for cumulative standard deductions has been adjusted as follows:
    • Cumulative Standard Deduction = Current-period Standard Deduction + Prior-period Cumulative Standard Deduction
    • The deduction will no longer be calculated based on the employee’s entry or exit date

Impact on Post-Resignation Payments

Another important change affects employees who receive payments after leaving the company. When special additional deductions are pre-filled, the system now uses the "supplementary wage tax period" as the end month for calculating deductions, potentially increasing the deduction amount for that period compared to the old rule.

What This Means for SAP Users

The payroll calculation logic for the cumulative standard deduction will be impacted, particularly for new hires and terminated employees.

SAP is introducing a switch to help customers implement these changes, utilizing existing fields 'Tax Entry Date' in Infotype 0531 to cover the requirements for cumulative standard deduction and wage type /4K* to fulfill the requirements for Special Additional Deduction.

Use Case Analysis

CASE 1 - ON THE JOB (CONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A started work in January (the entry date is 2026/01/01), the salary was not paid in January. The company has zero tax declaration in January and normal tax declaration in February. (Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, employee A in the period of February can use 2* 5000 as the cumulative deduction expense.

The cumulative deduction 10000 = the current deduction 5000 + the previous deduction 5000.

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: There is no more action need to be done.

  2. SAD Calculation: The record of IT3422, wage type /4J * and /4K* can still be used to record the SAD amount.

  3. Pay in next month: There is no more action need to be done for tax exemption.

CASE 2 - ON THE JOB (DISCONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A started work in January (the entry date is 2026/01/01), but since the salary was not paid in January, the company declared personal income tax for employee A from February of the month when the salary was paid. The period of February can be used. (Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, if the company directly starts to declare personal income tax for it from February, only one 5000 can be used as the cumulative deduction.

The cumulative deduction 5000 = the current deduction 5000 + the previous cumulative deduction 0.

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: Delimit from Feb in IT0531 and input the tax entry date as the begin date of Feb.

  2. SI/PHF and Income cumulation: Tick the cumulation reset in delimit record of Feb.

  3. SAD Calculation: Wage type /4K* is recommended to be used to record the SAD amount.

    For example, IT0015 in Feb: /4K* = 2000(Monthly SAD Amount = 1000)

  4. Pay in next month: The actual tax date need to input in the tax entry date, cumulation reset need to be ticked

CASE 3 - LEAVE THE COMPANY (CONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A’s last working day in company is 17 May 2026, and outstanding payments (e.g. leave encashment) are processed through the June payroll. (Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, the cumulative deduction is 30,000 (5,000 × 6 months).

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: There is no more action need to be done.
  2. SAD Calculation: The record of IT3422, wage type /4J * and /4K* can still be used to record the SAD amount.
  3. Pay in next month: There is no more action need to be done for tax exemption.

CASE 4 - LEAVE THE COMPANY (DISCONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A’s last working day in company is 17 May 2026, he/she received the following sequence payment in the following month:

June 2026: Leave encashment

July 2026: Other allowance

Aug 2026: No payment, No tax reporting

Sep 2026: Other allowance

(Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, the cumulative deduction for different month is as follows:

June 2026: Leave encashment: Tax exemption = 30000 (5,000 *6)

July 2026: Other allowance: Tax exemption = 35000 (5,000 *7)

Aug 2026: No Tax exemption

Sep 2026: Other allowance: Tax exemption = 5000 (5,000 *1)

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: There is no more action need to be done from Jun to Aug.
    Delimit from Sep in IT0531 and input the tax entry date and tick cumulation reset checkbox.
    (cumulation reset is set for SI/PHF and income cumulation)

  2. SI/PHF and Income cumulation: Tick the cumulation reset in delimit record of Sep.

  3. SAD Calculation: The record of IT3422, wage type /4J * and /4K* can still be used to record the SAD amount from June to July.
    Wage type /4K* is recommended to be used to record the SAD amount for September.
    For example, IT0015 in Sep: /4K* = 9000 (Monthly SAD Amount = 1000)

  4. Pay in next month: There is no more action need to be done for tax exemption from Jun to Aug. Delimit from Sep in IT0531 and input the tax entry date and tick cumulation reset checkbox. The actual tax date need to input in the tax entry date.

CASE 5 - REHIRE (CONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A is terminated in Oct and rehired in Nov. Run the payroll for Nov. (Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, the cumulative deduction is 55,000 (5,000 × 11 months).

Scenario 1: If you don't maintain tax entry date in the Jan record of IT0531

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: Delimit from Nov in IT0531 and input the tax entry date as begin date of 2026.

    If the employee is hired in the middle of year, such as 20260801. The tax entry date needs to set as hire date of 2026.

  2. SAD Calculation: The record of IT3422, wage type /4J * and /4K* can still be used to record the SAD amount.

  3. Pay in next month: The actual tax begin date needs to input in the tax entry date.

Scenario 2: If you maintain tax entry date in the Jan record of IT0531

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: There is no more action need to be done in Nov.
  2. SAD Calculation: The record of IT3422, wage type /4J* and /4K* can still be used to record the SAD amount.
  3. Pay in next month: There is no more action need to be done in Nov.

CASE 6 - REHIRE (DISCONTINUOUS DECLARATION)

Employee A is terminated in Oct and rehired in Dec. Run the payroll for Dec. (Employee A enjoys normal salary or belongs to intern in the system.)

Based on the new rule, the cumulative deduction is 5,000 (5,000 × 1 months).

Scenario 1: If you don't maintain tax entry date in the Jan record of IT0531

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: There is no more action need to be done.

  2. SI/PHF and Income cumulation: Delimit from Dec in IT0531 and tick the cumulation reset.

  3. SAD Calculation: Wage type /4K* is recommended to be used to record the SAD amount.
    For example, IT0015 in Dec: /4K* = 12000 (Monthly SAD Amount = 1000)

  4. Pay in next month: There is no more action need to be done for tax exemption. Tick cumulation reset from Dec.

Scenario 2: If you maintain tax entry date in the Jan record of IT0531

[Solution Suggestion]

  1. Exemption Calculation: Delimit from Dec in IT0531 and input the tax entry date as this tax month.

  2. SI/PHF and Income cumulation: Delimit from Dec in IT0531 and tick the cumulation reset.

  3. SAD Calculation: Wage type /4K* is recommended to be used to record the SAD amount.
    For example, IT0015 in Dec: /4K* = 12000 (Monthly SAD Amount = 1000)

  4. Pay in next month: Delimit from Dec in IT0531, input tax entry date and tick the cumulation reset.

For the latest updates regarding this regulatory change, please visit the link.

 

Terminology:

SAD - Special Additional Deduction

 

Resources and Support

For more detailed information of regulatory change released by SAP Globalization, and further collaboration and support on HCM localization for Greater China, please visit the provided links:

Regulatory Change Manager

Greater China - Human Capital Management Localization

 

Disclaimer:
SAP is not a law firm and is not able to provide any legal advice.
This blog, or any related comment, SAP's strategy and possible future developments, products and or platforms directions and functionality are all subject to change and may be changed at any time for any reason without notice.
This blog is not a commitment, promise or legal obligation to deliver any material, code or functionality.
This blog is provided without a warranty of any kind, either express or implied, including but not limited to, the implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, or non-infringement.

entry.title: Traditional Preventive Maintenance(BJ2) Vs Phase Based Proactive Maintenance(4HI)
entry.content:
Comparison Between Preventive and Proactive Maintenance

 

Maintenance strategies are essential for enhancing equipment reliability, minimizing downtime, and managing operational expenses. Two of the most commonly adopted methods are preventive maintenance and proactive maintenance. Although the terms are occasionally confused, they signify distinct philosophies and varying levels of advancement in maintenance management. This blog examines the primary differences between preventive and proactive maintenance, their advantages, drawbacks, and appropriate contexts for their implementation.

 

What Is Preventive Maintenance?
Preventive maintenance is a planned, time-based maintenance strategy. Regardless of the actual state of the equipment, tasks like servicing, lubrication, inspections, and part replacements are carried out at predetermined intervals.

Key Features of Preventive Maintenance:
 Aims to reduce the likelihood of failure
 Reactive to known wear patterns
 Easy to plan and implement
 Reduces unexpected breakdowns
 Extends equipment lifespan compared to reactive maintenance
 Improves safety and compliance

Limitations:
 May result in needless maintenance
 Does not address root causes of failures
 May still allow unexpected breakdowns
 May increase labour and spare-part costs

Preventive Flow

 

 

 

 

 

What Is Proactive Maintenance?

Proactive maintenance focuses on eliminating the root causes of equipment failure before problems occur. Instead of asking when maintenance should be performed, it asks why failures happen and how they can be prevented permanently.

Key Characteristics of Proactive Maintenance

Condition-based and data-driven

Uses root cause analysis (RCA)

Relies on monitoring tools (vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography)

Emphasizes design and process improvements

Advantages

Reduces recurring failures

Minimizes downtime and maintenance costs

Improves long-term reliability

Supports continuous improvement

Limitations

Higher upfront cost

Requires skilled personnel and advanced tools

More complex to implement

Needs organizational commitment

 

Proactive Flow

 

Phase Based Process – Proactive Maintenance

The Phase Based Process is divided into distinct steps which eases the end-user to view the order/notification in a granular view.

Each Phase has its own subphases/sub steps in Proactive maintenance that are mentioned below:

  1. Preparation
  2. Scheduling
  3. Execution
  4. Post-Execution
  5. Completion

 

Preparation:

Once an order is approved for execution and released, it is set to In Preparation and passed to the Preparation phase.

In this phase, the maintenance planner divides the work, resources, spare parts & services required.

By utilising the Manage Maintenance Planning Buckets Fiori application the planner segregates the work orders/planned shutdown activities based upon the type/duration of the maintenance process. Once orders are assigned to planning bucket scope, the list of orders can be reviewed with Manage Maintenance Backlog application.

This provides a detailed scope on the orders like consumables, non-stock components, resources & services

Once the maintenance planner is confident that all the required resources will be available on the required dates, they can select individual orders, choose Submit for Scheduling and thereby pass them on to the Scheduling phase.

 

 

 

 

Scheduling:

During this phase, the planner or supervisor evaluates the right work center is allocated to the right orders and time.

Note: The Operation Dispatch can also be done by Resource Scheduling. If MRS is excluded from the scope, then the operations/orders must be dispatched manually for collective or individual orders.

 

 

 

 

Check out how SAP MRS is used for Dispatching operations: Introducing the Phase Model

 

Execution:

The operations dispatched by the planner or supervisor are visible in Perform Maintenance Jobs application to the technician, who performs the assigned task and record time confirmation.

Upon successfully executing the jobs, the operations are set to subphase Work Finished.

 

 

 

Post Execution:

Post the time confirmation of proposed tasks with recommendations proposed. The order subphase is set to Main Work Completed which enables the Post Execution phase using Find Maintenance Order/Change Maintenance Order application.

Once the tasks are completed, services PR’s, stock components are utilised in the maintenance order, supervisor evaluates the maintenance cost, failure data and set the work order to Technically complete.

 

 

Completion:

Once the maintenance order is set to technically complete, all the postings related to order are closed including assigned notification(s) which are set to completion state.

During this phase, the financial controller reviews the maintenance order and settle the cost against the maintenance order and now the maintenance order is set to its final status Closed (Order) subphase.

 

 

 

Note: Furthermore, the Maintenance Planner/supervisor/Manager can analyse the cost and damages due to which the maintenance has been triggered using Maintenance Order Cost & Technical Object Damages Fiori application.

 

                  

 

 

 

 

 

Preventive vs Proactive Maintenance: Key Differences

Aspect

Preventive Maintenance

Proactive Maintenance

Approach

Planned maintenance

Planned maintenance

Focus

Preventing failure

Eliminating root causes

Effectiveness

Moderate

High

Long-Term Reliability

Improved

Optimized

Sub-Phases

Not included

Included

Application

GUI tiles

Enhanced web dynpro application



When to Use Each Strategy

  • Preventive maintenance is ideal for:
    • Organizations with limited resources
    • Non-critical equipment
    • Early stages of maintenance maturity
  • Proactive maintenance is best for:
    • Critical assets
    • High-cost downtime environments
    • Organizations aiming for operational excellence

In reality, a lot of businesses employ a hybrid strategy, beginning with preventive maintenance and progressively incorporating proactive methods as knowledge, resources, and skills increase.

Conclusion
     Proactive maintenance goes a step further by addressing the underlying causes of equipment problems, whereas preventive maintenance reduces failures through planned activities. While proactive maintenance offers better long-term reliability and cost savings, preventive maintenance is simpler to implement and provides immediate benefits. Budget, organizational maturity, and asset criticality all play a role in selecting the best approach, or a combination of the two.

entry.title: Future SAP apps will be transient - prove me wrong!
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • What is a transient app
  • Prompting
  • Benefit to user
  • Objections and Concerns
  • Cost of code
  • Training
  • Consistency across users and time
  • Scenario
  • Traditional apps (today)
  • Transient apps (future)
  • Summary

Introduction

Predicting the future is a fool’s errand! Things change at a fast pace, so we generally do not try and predict the winner of a sporting tournament 10 years in advance – unless we’re trying to stoke discussion. That’s my goal with this post!

 

What is a transient app

A transient app is an app that is written on-the-fly and lasts only during the session in which it is used. It is then removed from all memory as if it never existed. The concept of a transient app arose in the AI community around 2024/25 and is well described by a comparison to traditional apps as follows:

 

If a traditional app is like a restaurant you must travel to, a transient app is like a personal chef who appears in your kitchen, cooks exactly what you want, and cleans up so perfectly you wouldn't know they were there.

 

In the general world of AI, a transient app can pull data from various sources and so is not restricted to a single system like SAP.  Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP) is currently the best example of how this is done. We will, however, assume an entirely SAP ecosphere. Hence, we are considering what could be called SAP transient apps – Lets call them STAs for short.

Prompting

Prompting is an important concept in the world of AI including our future STA scenario. There must always be an interface with this SAP AI we’re imagining where a user can give “prompts”. This may be text-based (probably how you currently use your favourite LLM) or a verbal prompt or something else ( Neuralink?  😯 ) .

 

Benefit to user

For now, let’s ignore all objections to whether an STA is possible and look only at why a user would even want such a thing. Why not just have a standard interface that everyone uses i.e. what we have today?

Let’s look at some reasons using the simple example of a timesheet STA:

  1. The AI will learn to know what a user’s taste is in terms of UI. This is tweaked as user’s respond with prompts informing how a presented UI can be improved . One user may prefer a table like interface . Another might prefer tabs for each day with various colours, public holidays flashing, birthday reminders etc. Yet another might prefer a more wizard-like approach. Remember this is a simple example. A more complicated app will have more possible variations.

      2.  A UI may not be needed at all!  A user may be happy to give the prompt “log that I worked on all workdays this week”.  A UI-less STA can be generated that does this task. I'd use this option!

 

Objections and Concerns

This is the big one! Why STAs might seem like a pipedream – and why I think they are not.

 

Cost of code

The most fundamental hurdle may be our understanding of the cost of code. In our current world code requires human expertise and therefore has dollar costs and time costs. It is too valuable to throw out once its created.

 

Think of the darning kits that some of us can remember being around many years ago. They contained what was needed to repair a hole in a sock. Once socks became cheaper it became easier to “just buy a new pair” than sit down and spend time repairing. Just as socks have lost their value ( but not importance ) to us,  AI will make code less valuable ( but still important ) because it’s both quick and cheap to create.

 

This concept of code being created instantly is reminiscent of SAP’s move to HANA that imagined a world where there were no time costs to access data. Turns out that we still need to care about optimisation in DB selects because the time costs are not absolutely zero. However, the speed is sufficiently fast that our S4 ecosphere works well with the reduced attention on data select loads. No doubt there will be such nuances ahead where we find code to be not entirely “free and instant” but close enough to make a difference.

 

Training

How do you train people to use something when you don’t know exactly how the UI they will use looks? I’ll make the answer to this one short. The user can be walked through any given UI presented to the them by the AI itself (like a chatbot). On the first few uses of a UI to do a particular task they will probably request changes to the UI to suite their preferences. This will be remembered by the AI so that the user is not always seeing a different UI for a task that is done repeatedly. Even a brand-new task can result in a UI based on preferences requested by the user on previous tasks e.g. a user might prefer that wizard style flow in the UI. The AI gets to know you!

 

Consistency across users and time        

This is the question of whether one transient app is doing exactly the same thing as another transient app. It is a question more likely to be asked by an SAP expert than a user.

How do we know:

  1. That any 2 users are subject to the exact same business rules if they are using different STAs?
  2. Why certain data was changed in a certain way at a certain point in time? How  can we reproduce that to either audit or debug an issue if the STA that created the data is gone?

These concerns arise because we think in terms of traditional apps (whether Fiori apps, Web Dynpro apps or WEBGUI transactions).

Our traditional apps have business rules embedded in them! Even though we claim to abstract them away using BRF or something else the reality is that they are embedded in our code throughout. Let’s look at a simple example to contrast the current traditional world and the transitional world of the future.

 

Scenario

Materials of type “Dangerous Goods” will be added to the system. Only users with the role DGA (Dangerous Goods Approved) will be able to work with these materials. Anywhere, such a material is added to a document (like when creating a Purchase Order) the user must for legal reasons check a checkbox saying they have considered storage implications.

 

Traditional apps (today)

The Functional consultant will put the scenario above in a spec. They will then track down all UIs that allow a user  to create a document that uses materials. They may find (for our example) 3 such apps (the PO create Fiori app, a PO create transaction and a Stock Transfer Order app to move material between warehouses). They will then instruct security to create the role and assign to a list of people. They will ask the technical team to restrict materials available in the 3 apps to non-dangerous goods for unauthorised users – this can be done on the backend for all 3 apps (exits in DPC classes, BADIs, implicit enhancements etc).

On the frontend the checkbox must be added whenever a dangerous good is selected by authorised users and this must be checked before the frontend allows save/submit of the document.

Note that this new requirement is embedded into both backend and frontend code! If a new custom app is created that works with materials in a similar way, the functional consultant will need to remember this requirement to add it in. If it is not the same functional consultant hopefully, they’ve read a document somewhere that told them of this requirement (or it was in a knowledge transfer 😆😆).

Transient apps (future)

A functional consultant will declare the new requirement via an AI prompt. The prompt will basically be the scenario paragraph above. When the AI then asks exactly which users should have the new authorisation for dangerous goods the repsonse could be something like  “everyone in the current role ABC plus those in role DEF but don’t make a composite of these roles as this might change. Just create a new role DGA containing the current union of ABC and DEF”.

The rules will be extracted completely into a rules engine. This rules engine does not need to be human readable (like a BRF). Any authorised person can query this rules engine, and the results will be presented in a transient SAP app which will not only be human readable but optimised for that particular user's taste.

Any user creating a PO, say, will be subject to the rules and they will be enforced by the transient PO Creation app.

 

Summary

We see a secret to the transient app – the business rules are completely abstracted. We are left with only 3 persisting objects:

  1. Data
  2. Business engine (containing business rules & config)
  3. AI engine

The apps themselves don’t “live” anywhere. Some data and all business rules are timestamped. That way we could reproduce a transient app the way it would have run 2 years ago. Sure, the colours and other preferences may look different to what it did to the user that did something 2 years ago but the resulting behaviour is identical. 

Future SAP users will not be content with a prescribed app layout combined with a handful of themes like Quartz Light or Horizon Dark. They will insist on transient apps creating UIs that precisely match their personal preferences!

 

OK let's see how that ages...
